chunk_summary: Guidelines for starting complementary feeding at 6 months, focusing on developmental readiness, iron-rich first foods, and safe practices to support nutrition and skill-building in infants.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical signs of developmental readiness for complementary feeding.
Identify appropriate first foods and their preparation for infants starting at 6 months.
Recognize the risks of early or delayed complementary feeding and how to mitigate them.
Learn safe practices for introducing allergenic foods and avoiding harmful substances.
key_facts: Complementary feeding should start after 6 months (around 180 days) while continuing breastfeeding.
Developmental readiness includes sitting with support, steady head control, reduced tongue-thrust reflex, and interest in family food.
Delaying complementary feeding beyond 7 months increases the risk of growth faltering and iron deficiency.
First foods should be thick, energy-dense, and iron-rich, such as rice porridge, mashed khichdi, or ragi porridge enriched with ghee or oil.
Avoid honey in the first year due to botulism risk and sugar-sweetened drinks or fruit juice to prevent poor nutrition.
Introduce allergenic foods like egg or peanut in age-safe forms (e.g., cooked egg, peanut powder) without unnecessary delays.
reference_quote: Start complementary feeding after completion of 6 months (around 180 days) while continuing breastfeeding. Readiness is assessed clinically: the infant can sit with support and hold the head steady, shows interest in family food, and the tongue-thrust reflex is reduced so food is not automatically pushed out.
document_key_topics: Risks of early or delayed complementary feeding
